reason: The paper explicitly ranks RSF features "based on their importance values," citing Ishwaran's high-dimensional RSF variable-selection paper and Nembrini et al.'s "The revival of the Gini importance?" as its methodological basis (the latter specifically concerns Gini/impurity importance and its biases), indicating an impurity-type (MDI) measure drove the feature-removal step. This ranking is central to a stated conclusion: "all clinical features were removed from the combined model" during optimization, and the Discussion states "clinical features used in this work added no additional benefit," a claim used to justify design choices for future models. The only corroboration offered is that the final combined (post-removal) model achieves AUC 0.78-0.80 on the held-out validation cohort; per the task's own criterion this does not validate the ranking/removal decision itself, since no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or explicit ablation re-adding clinical features was reported. With 71 highly correlated continuous variability metrics competing against six clinically mixed-type (including binary) features, impurity importance's known scale/cardinality and correlation-driven inflation could plausibly have overstated variability importance and understated clinical feature importance, so the flag survives adversarial review.
